heading: TONUS SECUNDUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the Tonus Secundus with the first chapter, "Quid sit anima" (What is the soul), which pivots from the soul's consonance with the body and virtues (treated earlier) to the question of its proportion with all intelligible things. Zorzi dismisses Aristotle's two definitions of the soul — as "actus corporis physici, organici, potentia vitam habentis" and as "principium sentiendi, intelligendi, et secundum locum movendi" — as insufficient to explain how the soul can understand all things. He appeals instead to Plato (Laws X) and the Neoplatonist Proclus, who likewise judged most attempts to define the soul unsatisfactory, while noting that Plato himself refused to speak openly on the matter, considering it among the most difficult and hidden of subjects.
essay:
## Overview and Structural Position

This brief but programmatically important passage marks the opening of the *Tonus Secundus*, the second major tonal division of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The chapter heading — *Quid sit anima* ("What is the soul") — announces a transition in Zorzi's inquiry. Having previously examined the soul's consonance (*consonantia*) with the body as its form, and with the virtues as their subject, Zorzi now turns to the deeper metaphysical question: what proportion does the soul bear with *all things* (*omnes res*) that it is capable of understanding?

## The Inadequacy of Aristotelian Definitions

Zorzi opens with a pointed critique of Aristotle. He dismisses what he calls the "most widely received definition" (*iam vulgatissima diffinitio*):

> anima est actus corporis physici, organici, potentia vitam habentis

<cite>p. 2098</cite>

This Aristotelian formula — the soul as the actuality of a potentially living, organized physical body — is rejected not on ontological grounds per se, but on explanatory grounds: it tells us only that the soul is the mover of the body (*quod anima est ipsius corporis motrix*), and gives us no purchase on the question of how the soul is able to understand all things (*quomodo possit omnia intelligere*).

Zorzi also dismisses Aristotle's second definition:

> Anima est principium sentiendi, intelligendi, et secundum locum movendi

<cite>p. 2098</cite>

While this does name the soul as the principle of understanding, it still fails — in Zorzi's reading — to explain the *manner* in which the soul achieves this universal comprehension. He notes, charitably, that even Aristotle admitted (*ipso eodem teste*) that knowledge of the soul belongs to the most difficult class of problems.

## Appeal to Plato and the Neoplatonist Tradition

Zorzi roots Aristotle's difficulty in a dependency on Plato himself. Aristotle drew from Plato, who in *Laws* X had written:

> Qualis anima sit, et quam vim habeat, omnes ferme ignorasse videntur

<cite>p. 2098–2099</cite>

This citation of *Laws* X is characteristic of Zorzi's method: he turns to the late Plato not for the doctrine itself, but to authorize the apophatic posture that surrounds it — nearly everyone has been ignorant of what the soul is and what power it possesses. The implication is that Aristotelian definition-making misses the depth of Platonic humility on the subject.

Yet Plato did provide, Zorzi insists, a *doctrina* from which we can more easily investigate the soul's *symbolum* (its symbolic or analogical signature) and its proportion with all things (*proportionem cum rebus omnibus*). The key technical term here — *symbolum* — carries weight within the Neoplatonic tradition, indicating a formal or analogical correspondence rather than a simple causal relation. Zorzi is setting up the soul not as a substance merely defined by its relation to body, but as a kind of mirror or harmonic register capable of reflecting all of reality.

## Plato's Deliberate Obscurity and the Role of Proclus

An important methodological note follows. Plato himself, Zorzi observes, did not wish to speak openly (*aperto sermone*) about the soul's business, considering it *difficillimum* and *pene omnibus occultum* — most difficult and nearly hidden from all. This meant that his disciples (*discipuli*) defined the soul in varied and divergent ways — a multiplicity Zorzi had catalogued earlier in the first volume.

Zorzi invokes Proclus as the referee of these competing interpretations, noting that Proclus rejected many who attempted to explain Plato's meaning about the soul. The reference is to Proclus's commentary tradition — most likely the *Commentary on the Timaeus* or *Commentary on the Parmenides* — where Proclus systematically surveys and critiques earlier Platonic interpreters. The passage breaks off at a pivotal moment (with "nec ipse tamen") suggesting that even Proclus, despite his critical rejections, was not himself fully adequate to the task.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chapter opening performs a characteristic Zorzian move: clearing the Aristotelian ground in order to make room for a Platonic-Neoplatonist-Kabbalistic synthesis. The question *quomodo possit omnia intelligere* — how the soul can understand all things — is precisely the question that Zorzi's broader harmonic cosmology is designed to answer. The soul, as a microcosmic harmonic structure, must possess within itself a formal proportion to every level of being; this is why its definition requires not Aristotle's actuality-of-body formula, but something more like the Neoplatonic doctrine of the soul as a self-moving, self-reflexive totality.

The mention of the soul's *symbolum* points forward to the sefirotic and harmonic vocabulary Zorzi will deploy. The term also connects to the Kabbalistic notion that each level of the cosmic hierarchy bears formal traces (*reshimot*) of the levels above it, enabling a participatory epistemology in which like knows like across the chain of being. This is the metaphysical infrastructure Zorzi is laying in this opening chapter.
